Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content: The player moved RIGHT x 4. Let me check the current state to see where the player is now and what the W bar position is. The animation_changed_bbox is [36, 36, 39, 43], which is the player region (rows 36-39, cols 36-43).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g', 'G']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii around player and frames
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nRegion (rows 20-42, cols 22-46):")
for i in range(20, 42):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][22:46]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

The player moved RIGHT to cols 40-42, rows 36-39. The W bar is at col 43 (RIGHT of player). So the W bar is now on the RIGHT side.
The charcoal frames are at rows 20-23 cols 28-31 and rows 24-27 cols 36-39. Both are above the player (rows 36-39).
The W bar is on the RIGHT side, pointing RIGHT. The SPACE shot will hit frames to the RIGHT of the player. But the charcoal frames are above the player, not to the RIGHT.
So the SPACE shot won't hit the charcoal frames because they're not in the same row range.
I need to get the W bar to point UP to hit the charcoal frames above. To do that, I need to move UP and get blocked.
Looking at the ascii, the bottom frame is at rows 28-31, cols 28-39. The player is at rows 36-39, cols 40-42. So the bottom frame is above and LEFT of the player. If I move UP, I won't hit the bottom frame because it's to the LEFT of me.
Actually, the player is at cols 40-42, and the bottom frame is at cols 28-39. So the player is to the RIGHT of the bottom frame. If I move UP, I won't hit the bottom frame.
Let me try moving UP x 4 to get to rows 32-35, and then see what happens. The W bar might switch to the TOP side.
Actually, I realize I've been going in circles. Let me think about the game mechanics more carefully.
The W bar switches sides based on the player's movement direction when blocked. When I move RIGHT, the W bar switches to the RIGHT side. When I move DOWN, the W bar switches to the BOTTOM side. When I move UP, the W bar switches to the TOP side. When I move LEFT, the W bar switches to the LEFT side.
The SPACE shot converts the nearest frame in the direction of the W bar to dark gray (or white?).
Looking at the current state:
- Player at rows 36-39, cols 40-42
- W bar at col 43 (RIGHT)
- Charcoal frames at rows 20-23 cols 28-31 and rows 24-27 cols 36-39 (both above)
- Bottom frame at rows 28-31, cols 28-39 (above and LEFT)
To convert the charcoal frames to white, I need to:
1. Get the W bar to point UP (towards the charcoal frames)
2. Be in the same column range as the charcoal frames
3. Press SPACE
The charcoal frames are at cols 28-31 and cols 36-39. The player is at cols 40-42. So the player is to the RIGHT of both charcoal frames.
To get the W bar to point UP, I need to move UP and get blocked. But there's no obstacle above the player.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the game mechanics. The W bar seems to switch sides based on the player's last movement direction, not just when blocked. When I moved RIGHT, the W bar switched to the RIGHT side. When I moved DOWN, the W bar switched to the BOTTOM side. When I moved UP, the W bar switched to the TOP side.
So the W bar's direction is determined by the player's last movement direction. To get the W bar to point UP, I need to move UP.
But the SPACE shot hits frames in the direction of the W bar. If the W bar is on the TOP side (pointing UP), the SPACE shot will hit frames above the player. The charcoal frames are above the player, so the SPACE shot should hit them.
But the charcoal frames are at cols 28-31 and cols 36-39, and the player is at cols 40-42. So the charcoal frames are not directly above the player; they're above and to the LEFT.
Hmm, maybe the SPACE shot hits the nearest frame in the direction of the W bar, regardless of column alignment. Let me try moving UP to switch the W bar to the TOP side, and then press SPACE.
Actually, I realize I need to reconsider the goal. The goal is to convert all charcoal frames to white. Currently, there are two charcoal frames:
1. Top frame at rows 20-23, cols 28-31
2. Middle frame at rows 24-27, cols 36-39
And there's the bottom bar at rows 60-63, which is also charcoal. But this might be a HUD element, not a gameplay object.
To convert the charcoal frames to white, I need to shoot them with SPACE when the W bar is pointing in their direction.
Currently, the W bar is on the RIGHT side. To convert the charcoal frames, I need the W bar to point UP or LEFT (towards the charcoal frames).
Let me try moving UP to switch the W bar to the TOP side, and then press SPACE.
